## OQS Technical Steering Committee

- TSC chair transition: still seeking expressions of interest in a TSC co-chair. See issue [#246](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/246).
- PQCA mentorships: it is not too late to submit project proposals (see issue [#240](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/240)), but interested individuals and organizations should act soon.
- Congratulations to Rodrigo Martín, who is now a maintainer on the OQS-OpenSSL provider.

## liboqs

Douglas noted that after being out of the project for a few weeks he is getting back into liboqs and beginning to chip away at the variety of open issues and PRs that have built up, with the aim of landing what is ready. The plan for liboqs 0.16.0 is to first land the remaining high-priority items (notably the HQC update), then move toward a release candidate and start coordinating with downstream projects.

### HQC update (PR [#2328](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2328))

Bruce reported that the updated HQC reference implementation is on its way, so downstream projects should expect HQC to return to liboqs. Douglas noted that landing HQC was the last high-priority item identified for the next liboqs release; once the HQC PR is merged he intends to move toward a release candidate for liboqs 0.16.0 and begin coordinating with downstream projects.

### Classic McEliece and the PQClean wind-down (issue [#2341](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2341))

Rodrigo raised the question of what to do about Classic McEliece, whose current liboqs implementation comes from PQClean. PQClean support is winding down (around June 2026), and Classic McEliece is a particular concern because it is expected to be standardized by ISO, which could drive renewed interest at exactly the moment upstream support disappears.

Discussion points:

- Michael recalled the long-standing open issue on documenting algorithm support levels (issue [#2045](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2045), already on the 0.16.0 priority board) and suggested that simply stating clearly the status of Classic McEliece — and other algorithms — would let users know whether they are on their own or might want to help maintain it. This mirrors what happened with NTRU, where an interested party could in principle take ownership.
- Douglas pointed to the libmceliece implementation from the McEliece team itself (link shared by Bruce in chat). The license (public domain / MIT / others) is compatible with liboqs, so it could be used; however, Douglas reiterated his earlier assessment that the McEliece team is unlikely to provide active support to the OQS effort.
- Michael questioned whether liboqs should adopt an alternative implementation absent a concrete user asking for it via liboqs; users wanting Classic McEliece could be pointed at the upstream implementation directly.
- Consensus: rather than swap implementations now, get the algorithm support level table done properly and use it to clearly communicate the status of Classic McEliece (and other algorithms) to users. If concrete demand or a willing maintainer materializes, the situation can be revisited.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Make sure issue [#2045](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/issues/2045) (table of algorithm support levels) lands before the liboqs 0.16.0 release, and capture the Classic McEliece / PQClean wind-down situation in it.

## OQS-OpenSSL provider

### PR [#752](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/752) — Preserve property string for KEMs

Rodrigo reported that this PR (originally submitted by Norm) has been stale for about six weeks. Beyond the staleness itself, Rodrigo's deeper concern is that while reviewing the PR he noticed that some of its tests pass when he believes they should not. He is fairly confident this points to a real bug — possibly in the OQS provider, possibly in OpenSSL itself — that is more worrisome than the issue the PR was originally trying to address. The PR cannot be merged until this is resolved, because merging it could mask or introduce a real error.

Michael suggested separating the two concerns: file a dedicated issue that reproduces the surfaced behavior outside of Norm's PR, so that it can be discussed independently (potentially with input from the OpenSSL team) and used as a test case that the PR can then be measured against.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Open a separate issue reproducing the suspected bug surfaced by PR [#752](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/752), independent of the PR itself, so it can be triaged (and possibly raised with the OpenSSL team).

### Long-stale PRs ([#655](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/655), [#641](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/641))

Rodrigo noted that the other two open PRs in oqs-provider have been open for more than a year, predating his involvement. Michael had previously cleaned up many old PRs but left these two because he believes they contain a grain of value; he is fine with closing them and recreating them as `help wanted` issues. Rodrigo will take another look — he has examined one of them before but was blocked by lacking the infrastructure to test a particular build configuration, and has not yet looked at the other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Re-review oqs-provider PRs [#655](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/655) and [#641](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/pull/641) and decide whether to close them in favor of `help wanted` issues.

### Issue [#761](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/oqs-provider/issues/761) — TLS startup failed

Rodrigo is fairly confident this issue is not an oqs-provider bug but rather a misconstructed certificate on the user's side.

## OQS-OpenSSH

A user has been actively engaging with the OQS-OpenSSH repository and has filed three issues so far. One led to an SLH-DSA naming update (catching up to the provider); another is a documentation request; the third concerns OpenSSH-style certificates.

Specifically, the user tried to create post-quantum keys and elevate them to OpenSSH-style certificates (the bespoke OpenSSH certificate format, not X.509 — X.509 support would be via PKIX). Alex's recollection, corroborated by Andrew Younkers and consistent with what Alex saw last year while working with ML-DSA, is that certificate support was simply never added to OQS-OpenSSH for the post-quantum algorithms. Douglas's memory is fuzzy but does not recall any certificate-related work being done; Christian Paquin, who explored this area several years ago, did not recall details either.

Alex told the user this is his belief and plans, together with Andrew, to add it as a feature when they find time. The positive note: someone has found the repo, is using it, and is engaging constructively.
